a: NOTA (None of the Above) is a ballot option on EVMs in India. If you select NOTA, you indicate none of the candidates are acceptable; it does not invalidate the election or trigger a re-poll. It is a symbolic protest vote.

q: How do I register as a new voter?
a: Use Form 6 on the Voter Helpline App or https://voters.eci.gov.in/ with age and address proof, or submit offline to your BLO/ERO.
Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) record your vote electronically. VVPAT prints a slip visible for 7 seconds for verification. ECI trains officers on secure handling and counting procedures.
Indian citizens aged 18+ on the qualifying date, ordinarily resident in the constituency, and not disqualified under law may register and vote.

Overseas Indians can register via Form 6A if eligible; they vote in person at their designated polling station in India (not postal for Lok Sabha general elections in normal rules—verify current ECI guidance).

q: What ID can I use at the polling booth?
a: EPIC is common but ECI allows 12 alternative photo IDs including Aadhaar, passport, driving licence, and others listed on the ECI website.

q: What is the Model Code of Conduct?
a: MCC is ECI guidelines for fair campaigning: restrictions on new announcements, misuse of official machinery, and corrupt practices during elections.
Votes are counted under ECI supervision with strongrooms, VVPAT slips sampled per ECI protocol, and results declared officially.

q: What is the voter helpline number?
a: National voter helpline: 1950. You can also use the Voter Helpline mobile app for registration status and booth information.
Certain service voters, special categories, and provisions defined by ECI/law may use postal ballot; check current ECI notifications for your category.
EPIC (Electors Photo Identity Card) is your voter ID number on the card issued by ECI; it helps identify you on the electoral roll.
Large states may hold polling in multiple phases for security and logistics; dates are announced in the ECI election schedule.

q: How do I object to a wrong entry in the roll?
a: Use Form 7 as prescribed by ECI and follow your CEO/ERO office process with supporting evidence.

q: How do I correct my voter details?
a: Use Form 8 for corrections or shifting within rules; online via voters portal or offline through BLO/ERO.
Local administration may impose orders restricting gatherings; always follow lawful orders and verify with local authorities—this is general civic information, not legal advice.
ECI may establish all-women managed booths in some areas for accessibility and comfort—availability varies by election and state.

q: What facilities exist for voters with disabilities?
a: ECI promotes ramps, wheelchairs, volunteers, and accessible materials; report gaps to the presiding officer or helpline 1950.
Higher turnout strengthens representative legitimacy; every eligible vote shapes outcomes within the constitutional framework.
The Election Commission of India conducts Lok Sabha and Assembly elections; State Election Commissions conduct local body polls per constitutional allocation.

q: Can I verify VVPAT with my vote?
a: VVPAT displays a paper slip for verification; ECI defines verification protocols during counting and audits—follow on-booth instructions from officials.
Lok Sabha is the lower house of India's Parliament with 543 elected seats. Members are directly elected by citizens for a five-year term through general elections conducted by ECI.
Rajya Sabha is the upper house of Parliament with up to 245 members. Members are indirectly elected by state legislators for six-year terms, with one-third retiring every two years.
State Legislative Assemblies (Vidhan Sabha) are the directly elected legislative bodies at the state level. Members serve five-year terms and elections are conducted by ECI.
Panchayat elections choose representatives for the three-tier local governance system (village, block, and district levels). These are conducted by State Election Commissions, not ECI.
Municipal elections choose representatives for urban local bodies such as municipal corporations, municipalities, and nagar panchayats. They are conducted by State Election Commissions.
A by-election is held when a seat in Lok Sabha or a state assembly becomes vacant mid-term due to death, resignation, or disqualification of the sitting member. ECI announces the schedule.

q: How is the Election Commission composed?
a: The Election Commission of India consists of the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) and two Election Commissioners, appointed by the President. All three have equal decision-making power.

q: What is the qualifying date for voter registration?
a: ECI has four qualifying dates per year: January 1, April 1, July 1, and October 1. You must be 18 years old on or before the qualifying date to be eligible for enrollment in that revision cycle.
Form 6A is used by overseas Indian (NRI) citizens to register as voters. Eligible NRIs must hold a valid Indian passport and can register for the constituency in their passport address.
Form 6B is used to link your Aadhaar number with your voter ID (EPIC). This helps ECI maintain a clean electoral roll by eliminating duplicate entries.
Form 8A is used for transposition of an entry within the same constituency when a voter shifts residence but stays in the same assembly segment. Submit it to your ERO or online via the voters portal.
SVEEP (Systematic Voters' Education and Electoral Participation) is ECI's flagship program to boost voter awareness, registration, and turnout through targeted outreach campaigns.
ECI may order a re-poll at specific booths if booth capture, EVM malfunction, or natural calamity disrupts the voting process. The decision rests solely with ECI based on field reports.

q: How do I file an MCC violation complaint?
a: Use the C-Vigil app to report Model Code of Conduct violations with photo or video evidence. You can also file a written complaint with the Returning Officer (RO) or District Election Officer (DEO).
ECI provides Braille-enabled EVMs with Braille labels on ballot units and dummy ballot papers for visually impaired voters. Presiding officers assist as needed at the booth.
Transgender citizens can select the third gender option on Form 6 during voter registration. ECI follows inclusive enrollment practices to ensure equal electoral participation.

q: What is home voting for elderly and PwD?
a: Voters aged 80 and above and persons with disabilities can apply for postal ballot facility through their BLO to vote from home. This must be requested before the deadline set by ECI.

q: What should I carry on polling day?
a: Carry a valid photo ID (EPIC or one of 12 ECI-approved alternatives), know your polling booth number, and do not bring mobile phones or cameras inside the voting compartment.

C-Vigil is ECI's citizen vigilance mobile app that lets anyone report Model Code of Conduct violations with geotagged photo or video evidence. Reports are routed to the nearest flying squad for action.
EVMs are sealed with multiple tamper-evident seals, and serial numbers are recorded in the presence of candidate agents. The sealed EVMs are stored in secure strongrooms under CCTV surveillance.
Counting agents are representatives appointed by candidates to observe the vote counting process. They ensure transparency and can raise objections on behalf of their candidate during counting.
Each polling booth uses one Ballot Unit (BU) and one Control Unit (CU). If candidates exceed 16, additional BUs are linked to the same CU to accommodate all names.
A mock poll is a mandatory test run of EVMs conducted at every booth before actual polling begins. A minimum of 50 votes are cast and verified to confirm the machines are working correctly.

q: What is green protocol in elections?
a: ECI's green protocol encourages eco-friendly election practices including reduced single-use plastic, digital campaigning over printed materials, and sustainable booth setup guidelines.

Postal ballots are available to service voters (armed forces, diplomats), senior citizens aged 80+, persons with disabilities, essential service workers on election duty, and preventive detention detainees.
Delimitation is the process of redrawing constituency boundaries by the Delimitation Commission based on the latest census data. It ensures roughly equal population representation per seat.

ECI allots election symbols to candidates. Recognized national and state parties have reserved symbols, while independent and unrecognized party candidates choose from a free symbols list maintained by ECI.

ECI sets maximum spending limits for candidates that vary by state and election type. For Lok Sabha elections the cap ranges from ₹75–95 lakh depending on the state. Candidates must file expenditure accounts.
An election petition is a legal challenge to election results filed in the High Court within 45 days of the result declaration. Grounds include corrupt practices or non-compliance with electoral law.

q: Can a voter's name be removed from the electoral roll?
a: Yes, a name can be removed via Form 7 objection, or purged by ERO if the voter is deceased, has shifted permanently, or holds duplicate entries. Removal follows a verification process.

q: Can someone contest from two constituencies?
a: Yes, a candidate may contest from up to two constituencies simultaneously. If elected from both, they must vacate one seat within 14 days of the result, as prescribed by law.
The anti-defection law under the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution disqualifies elected members who voluntarily give up party membership or vote against the party whip without permission.
ECI has constitutional authority for superintendence, direction, and control of elections. It can postpone or cancel polls, order re-polls, and enforce the Model Code of Conduct.
National Voters' Day is celebrated on January 25 every year since 2011 to mark ECI's founding anniversary and encourage electoral participation, especially among newly eligible young voters.

q: Can I use RTI for election information?
a: Yes, ECI and all election offices are public authorities under the Right to Information Act 2005. Citizens can file RTI requests for election-related data, expenditure records, and administrative decisions.
